distractors.text: Keep using the model given the results and prepare documentation to capture it retroactively.
distractors.trap: Good results don't justify bypassing governance.

explanation: Reflects the Business Environment / integrated change control discipline: all baseline deviations, even favorable ones, require formal assessment and authorization.

prompts: In execution, testers report a contractor merged the payment and user-profile modules, diverging from the architecture. Benchmarks show memory nearing the device limit, yet the merge saved two sprint days and pleases the sponsor pressing for release. Directing and managing the work, what should the project manager do next?

correct.text: Submit a corrective change request to the change control board before approving further coding.
correct.why: The unauthorized merge changes the baseline; the CCB must evaluate it before work proceeds either way.

distractors.text: Stop all work and order the contractor to revert to the separate modules.
distractors.trap: Unilateral reversal pre-empts the CCB's decision.

distractors.text: Run a workshop with the sponsor to rewrite performance objectives around the merged design.
distractors.trap: Adopts the unauthorized change as baseline, bypassing governance.

distractors.text: Authorize memory-optimization tasks and record the variance in lessons learned.
distractors.trap: Adds work to accommodate an unapproved change without evaluation.

explanation: Change performance domain: baseline changes must flow through integrated change control.

prompts: On a complex software project, unforeseen technical challenges cause a significant schedule deviation that threatens quality. Stakeholders allow no deadline extension or quality compromise. Which action best fits directing and managing the work?

correct.text: Perform integrated change control to assess the deviation's impact on outputs and determine adjustments.
correct.why: Integrated change control evaluates impact and defines adjustments without sacrificing quality.

distractors.text: Increase the team's working hours to accelerate and recover the schedule.
distractors.trap: Crashing without analysis ignores quality and burnout risk.

distractors.text: Outsource part of the work to a contractor without reviewing budget impact.
distractors.trap: Acts without assessing cost or impact.

distractors.text: Fast-track by running sequential activities in parallel without evaluating risk.
distractors.trap: Fast-tracking without risk analysis can worsen quality.

explanation: Change performance domain: assess impact through integrated change control before acting.

prompts: Late in developing the project management plan for an international infrastructure project, a team member notes that recently updated environmental regulations may no longer align with the project's approach. What is the most appropriate action?

correct.text: Review the updated regulations, assess current compliance, and adjust the project management plan accordingly.
correct.why: Assessing the specific gap and updating the plan keeps the project compliant without overreacting.

distractors.text: Direct the team to apply the new regulations next phase without checking current alignment.
distractors.trap: Acts without assessing where the plan is misaligned.

distractors.text: Halt all activities and conduct a full review of every environmental regulation with experts.
distractors.trap: Overreaction that needlessly delays the project.

distractors.text: Continue with the current plan and address compliance issues as they surface.
distractors.trap: Reactive; risks non-compliance and legal exposure.

explanation: Compliance and stewardship: proactively align the plan with current legal obligations.

topic: Handle unauthorized process change via change control
prompts: On an infrastructure upgrade, you notice the team has been using a modified, time-saving version of an authorized concrete-pour method that was never evaluated or approved through change control. Directing and managing the work, what should you do?

correct.text: Pause the modified approach, assess its impact, document it, and submit a change request for approval.
correct.why: Unauthorized changes must be stopped, evaluated, and routed through formal change control.

distractors.text: Have QA confirm it meets standards, then retroactively update documents to the actual practice.
distractors.trap: Still bypasses change control and ignores non-quality impacts.

distractors.text: Let the team continue and capture it as a lesson learned at project end.
distractors.trap: Leaves current risks unaddressed.

distractors.text: Adopt the modified method as the new standard across similar activities for efficiency.
distractors.trap: Expands an unapproved change, amplifying risk.

explanation: Change performance domain: evaluate unauthorized changes through integrated change control.

topic: Handling reported unethical behavior via proper process
prompts: As an ethical project leader, how should you respond when a team member reports observing unethical behavior by another team member?

correct.text: Conduct an objective investigation, involving appropriate stakeholders and following the organization's ethics policies.
correct.why: Ethical duty requires a fair, policy-based investigation of reported misconduct.

distractors.text: Report it to the sponsor and let them handle it, since it's not your responsibility.
distractors.trap: Abdicates the leader's ethical duty.

distractors.text: Ask the accused for their side and judge it on your personal assessment.
distractors.trap: Informal judgment invites bias and unfairness.

distractors.text: Tell the reporter to keep it confidential to protect team morale.
distractors.trap: Suppressing misconduct over transparency is unethical.

explanation: Stewardship principle: uphold ethics through a fair, policy-based investigation.

prompts: You learn that a team member has been sharing confidential project information with a competitor. As an ethical project leader, what should you do?

correct.text: Investigate the situation, gather the facts, and follow the organization's policies for handling the breach.
correct.why: PMI ethics require fact-based, fair handling of confidentiality breaches through established organizational procedures.

distractors.text: Report it to the member's supervisor and let them handle it as they see fit.
distractors.trap: Delegates accountability and risks inconsistent, non-policy handling of a serious breach.

distractors.text: Confront the member privately and warn them of consequences if it recurs.
distractors.trap: Skips due process and evidence; an informal warning may not address a real breach.

distractors.text: Keep it quiet to protect morale while quietly monitoring the person.
distractors.trap: Concealment ignores accountability and violates the code of ethics.

explanation: Reflects PMI's Code of Ethics (responsibility, honesty) and the Business Environment domain's compliance duty: act on facts within organizational policy.

prompts: As an Agile coach you work with a team that resists change and rarely identifies improvements in retrospectives. What approach best builds a continuous-improvement mindset?

correct.text: Encourage small, incremental improvements and celebrate progress to foster experimentation and learning.
correct.why: Small wins and psychological safety build a durable culture of continuous improvement.

distractors.text: Accept they aren't ready and let them keep current processes indefinitely.
distractors.trap: Abandons improvement and stalls the team's growth.

distractors.text: Impose a strict improvement process the team must follow exactly.
distractors.trap: Mandates breed resistance and lack of buy-in.

distractors.text: Insist on immediate, significant process changes despite their concerns.
distractors.trap: Overwhelms the team and heightens resistance.

explanation: Continuous improvement principle: advance value through incremental change and a learning culture.

topic: Action when a risk may breach the threshold
prompts: Stakeholders set a risk threshold for quality defects at 5.5%. A new supplier's materials show a 5.0% defect rate, but inconsistencies could push it to 6.0%. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Develop a mitigation plan to keep the defect rate below the threshold.
correct.why: The risk may breach the threshold, so proactively reduce probability or impact before it does.

distractors.text: Prepare a contingency plan for if defects significantly exceed the threshold.
distractors.trap: Reactive; waits for the breach instead of preventing it.

distractors.text: Stay with the supplier to avoid delay and cost, even if defects exceed the threshold.
distractors.trap: Knowingly accepting a breach violates the stakeholders' threshold.

distractors.text: Proceed as is, since 5.0% is currently within standards.
distractors.trap: Ignores the known potential to exceed the threshold.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: act proactively when exposure may cross a stakeholder-defined threshold.

topic: Recurring outages as a risk trigger
prompts: Deploying a cloud enterprise system, you notice the primary provider's brief service outages growing more frequent over two weeks. The team had flagged vendor reliability as a risk and documented conditions that would warrant the contingency plan. What do these outages represent?

correct.text: A risk trigger signaling the vendor-reliability risk may be materializing, so responses should be evaluated.
correct.why: A trigger is an observable warning sign that a known risk is about to occur.

distractors.text: A risk response requiring a schedule update and stakeholder communication.
distractors.trap: A response is an action taken, not the warning symptom itself.

distractors.text: A trigger meaning scope must be revised and alternate vendors formally contracted.
distractors.trap: A trigger prompts evaluation, not automatic scope or contracting actions.

distractors.text: A secondary risk arising from the original vendor selection.
distractors.trap: Secondary risks arise from responses, not from a flagged risk's symptoms.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: a risk trigger is a warning sign to evaluate the planned response.

prompts: How does risk attitude most influence the prioritization of project risks?

correct.text: It shapes the perceived importance of different risks.
correct.why: Risk attitude is subjective and colors which risks stakeholders judge most significant.

distractors.text: It mandates using quantitative risk analysis techniques.
distractors.trap: Method choice depends on data and need, not attitude.

distractors.text: It fixes the exact number of risks the project addresses.
distractors.trap: Risk count depends on many factors, not attitude.

distractors.text: It solely defines the project's overall risk tolerance threshold.
distractors.trap: Attitude influences but doesn't exclusively set thresholds.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: risk attitude subjectively shapes how stakeholders perceive and prioritize risk.

topic: Next step for newly identified secondary risks
prompts: You accept the risk of accelerating testing, knowing it may create code-quality defects and extra debugging. After making that decision, what is your immediate next step regarding the newly identified secondary risks?

correct.text: Assess the secondary risks and plan suitable responses.
correct.why: Secondary risks created by a response must be analyzed and given planned responses, proactively.

distractors.text: Run a full project audit to gauge the impact of all risks.
distractors.trap: Too heavy and slow for an immediate next step.

distractors.text: Focus on original risks and treat the secondary ones as less critical.
distractors.trap: Secondary risks can escalate and shouldn't be dismissed.

distractors.text: Keep monitoring the acceleration to ensure no further secondary risks arise.
distractors.trap: Monitoring alone doesn't plan responses for identified risks.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: secondary risks created by a response require their own assessment and response planning.

prompts: Which statement best characterizes the relationship between risk threshold and risk appetite in project risk management?

correct.text: Threshold is the maximum acceptable exposure for a specific risk category; appetite is the overall risk the organization will accept pursuing its objectives.
correct.why: Threshold is category-specific and measurable; appetite is the broad, organization-wide willingness to take risk.

distractors.text: Threshold is the minimum risk that must be maintained to meet objectives; appetite is the maximum acceptable overall project risk.
distractors.trap: Threshold is a maximum acceptable level, not a minimum to maintain.

distractors.text: Threshold and appetite are interchangeable, both meaning the maximum risk accepted for a project.
distractors.trap: They describe different levels; they are not synonyms.

distractors.text: Threshold is where risks become critical; appetite is where risks are insignificant and accepted.
distractors.trap: Mischaracterizes both as opposite cut-off points.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: threshold is the category-level limit; appetite is the organization's overall willingness to take risk.

prompts: You are classifying a newly identified event: a key competitor announced it is exiting the market segment, potentially letting your product capture share. Which PMI response strategy best ensures this beneficial outcome materializes?

correct.text: Exploit it, accelerating the launch and increasing marketing to secure the vacated position.
correct.why: Exploit takes definite action to make an opportunity certain to be realized.

distractors.text: Enhance it by adding development resources while monitoring for similar shifts.
distractors.trap: Enhance raises probability or impact but doesn't ensure it occurs.

distractors.text: Share it by partnering with a distributor to capture the extra share.
distractors.trap: Share gives control and benefit to a third party.

distractors.text: Mitigate it by analyzing the market and planning for a competitor return.
distractors.trap: Mitigation is a threat strategy, not an opportunity response.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: exploit is the opportunity response that makes a beneficial outcome certain.

prompts: On a coastal bridge project the team reinforced piers and bought storm-surge insurance. An audit finds a small remaining chance that unforeseen scour could undermine the foundations, now low probability with costs within contingency. How should this remaining threat be classified for monitoring?

correct.text: Treat it as residual risk and track it through scheduled reviews.
correct.why: Residual risk is the leftover exposure after responses, monitored within tolerance and contingency.

distractors.text: Call it a transferred risk since insurance exists and rely on the insurer's oversight.
distractors.trap: The team still retains exposure (deductibles, limits) to monitor.

distractors.text: Re-label it an accepted opportunity because contingency adds schedule flexibility.
distractors.trap: An undermining event is a threat, not an opportunity.

distractors.text: Label it a secondary risk and build a fresh mitigation plan.
distractors.trap: Secondary risks are created by responses; this is leftover, not new.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: residual risk is the remaining exposure after responses, tracked in normal reviews.

prompts: In project risk management, what should be done when a residual risk's impact would exceed the project's risk tolerance?

correct.text: Revise the risk responses to reduce the residual risk.
correct.why: Exceeding tolerance means the current responses are inadequate and must be strengthened.

distractors.text: Observe and monitor the residual risk as part of ongoing oversight.
distractors.trap: Monitoring alone leaves the intolerable exposure unaddressed.

distractors.text: Transfer ownership of the residual risk to an external entity.
distractors.trap: Not necessarily applicable or effective for this risk.

distractors.text: Accept the residual risk and proceed with the plan as defined.
distractors.trap: Accepting an intolerable exposure risks serious consequences.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: when residual risk exceeds tolerance, revise responses to bring it within limits.

prompts: A warehouse-automation risk log has three entries with similar probability-impact scores: (1) a fire inspection tomorrow by an inspector known for stop-work orders, (2) the software vendor may drop support in ~18 months, (3) energy costs may rise 11.5% next fiscal year. Which factor should drive handling the fire inspection first?

correct.text: The speed at which the fire inspection could materialize and disrupt operations sets it apart from the slower threats.
correct.why: With equal scores, risk urgency (velocity), how soon it can hit, drives the response sequence.

distractors.text: Its residual exposure after standard mitigation is lower than the others'.
distractors.trap: Post-mitigation residual doesn't set initial response sequencing.

distractors.text: Its cumulative probability plus proximity to milestones compounds baseline impact.
distractors.trap: Invented compound effect; the scores are already similar.

distractors.text: Its detectability index is higher, making it easier to monitor.
distractors.trap: Higher detectability gives more warning, lowering urgency.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: risk urgency (velocity) breaks ties when probability-impact scores are equal.

prompts: On an infrastructure upgrade, the PM notes heavy monsoon rainfall could delay progress and that rising humidity often precedes heavy rain. Which action best exemplifies using a risk trigger?

correct.text: Monitoring humidity levels as an indicator of impending heavy rainfall.
correct.why: A trigger is a measurable warning sign that a risk is about to occur.

distractors.text: Monitoring daily temperature swings to adjust construction schedules.
distractors.trap: Temperature isn't the identified precursor to the rainfall risk.

distractors.text: Reallocating funds to buy weather-resistant materials.
distractors.trap: That's mitigation, not monitoring a trigger.

distractors.text: Postponing all outdoor work until after monsoon season.
distractors.trap: That's avoidance, not use of a trigger.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: a risk trigger is a warning indicator that a risk is imminent.

prompts: A pharma company developing a new drug faces three risks: side effects (high impact, low probability), regulatory delays (likely, moderate impact), and manufacturing constraints (in between), with patent expiration approaching fast. Which concept should guide which risk is most urgent to handle?

correct.text: Risk velocity
correct.why: Velocity factors how quickly a risk would hit, critical given the looming patent expiration.

distractors.text: Risk severity, ranking risks solely by potential impact.
distractors.trap: Ignores probability and the time dimension.

distractors.text: Risk proximity, focusing only on near-term risks regardless of impact.
distractors.trap: May overlook high-impact risks and speed of effect.

distractors.text: Risk exposure, combining probability and impact.
distractors.trap: Omits the time-sensitivity the patent deadline creates.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: risk velocity captures how fast a risk impacts, key under time pressure.

prompts: What is the key difference between secondary risks and other project risks?

correct.text: Secondary risks arise as a direct result of implementing risk responses.
correct.why: They are new risks created by the response actions taken, not pre-existing ones.

distractors.text: They are identified during the initial risk assessment phase.
distractors.trap: Secondary risks emerge later, when responses are implemented.

distractors.text: They have a higher probability of occurring than primary risks.
distractors.trap: Their probability varies; no inherent higher likelihood.

distractors.text: They are risks transferred to external stakeholders for mitigation.
distractors.trap: Transfer is a response strategy, not the definition of secondary risk.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: secondary risks are byproducts of implementing risk responses.

prompts: During risk identification for a bank's infrastructure upgrade, the IT director is eager to adopt blockchain for its competitive edge, while the compliance officer keeps raising regulatory uncertainty and prefers audited, established methods. What does this difference in perspectives primarily demonstrate?

correct.text: Individual risk attitudes shaped by professional background and experience lead to different readings of the same uncertain event.
correct.why: Risk attitude varies by role and experience, producing different interpretations of identical uncertainty.

distractors.text: Perceptions reflect functional expertise and should be weighted by departmental budget.
distractors.trap: Budget size doesn't validate a risk perception.

distractors.text: Organizational tolerance thresholds should override individual stakeholder preferences.
distractors.trap: Sidesteps the point; it's about differing attitudes, not overriding them.

distractors.text: Technical stakeholders assess risk more accurately than compliance stakeholders.
distractors.trap: Neither role is inherently more accurate.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: stakeholder risk attitudes differ by background, shaping how the same uncertainty is interpreted.

prompts: Analyzing threats during Plan Risk Management, the team notes a critical software module depends on a legacy system that may become unsupported during the project. Which concept best explains why this should be documented and monitored as a risk?

correct.text: The dependency creates uncertainty that could adversely affect timeline and budget if legacy support ends unexpectedly.
correct.why: A risk is an uncertain event that, if it occurs, affects objectives, matching this dependency.

distractors.text: It's an assumption about system availability needing validation via stakeholder and technical review.
distractors.trap: It's uncertainty with impact, a risk, not merely an assumption.

distractors.text: It's a constraint limiting options, addressed by adjusting the scope baseline.
distractors.trap: It's an uncertain future event, not a fixed limitation.

distractors.text: It's a known issue to track in the issue log rather than assess as a risk.
distractors.trap: Issues are current problems; this is a future uncertainty.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain: a risk is an uncertain event with potential impact on objectives, unlike assumptions, constraints, or issues.

prompts: You have identified a potential risk that could impact the schedule and decide to set aside additional resources for it. This is called a ________ reserve.

correct.text: Contingency
correct.why: Contingency reserves cover identified risks (known unknowns) and sit within the cost baseline.

distractors.text: Management
distractors.trap: Management reserves are for unknown unknowns and need approval.

distractors.text: Buffer
distractors.trap: Generic term, not the PMI reserve for identified risks.

distractors.text: Emergency
distractors.trap: Not a standard PMI reserve term.

explanation: Risk / uncertainty domain (financial management): contingency reserves address identified, known-unknown risks within the baseline.

prompts: A junior team member asks how the project's contingency reserve relates to the cost baseline. Which statement is accurate?

correct.text: Contingency reserve sits within the cost baseline and the PM controls it to address identified risks.
correct.why: Contingency reserves for known risks are part of the cost baseline and spent at PM discretion.

distractors.text: It is held outside the cost baseline and needs sponsor approval before any funds are used.
distractors.trap: Confuses contingency reserve with management reserve.

distractors.text: It is added to the cost baseline only after execution begins and risks are validated.
distractors.trap: Contingency is baselined during planning, not added later.

distractors.text: It is set after the baseline is authorized and buffers both known and unknown risks.
distractors.trap: Unknown risks are covered by management reserve, not contingency.

explanation: Reflects the Uncertainty performance domain and cost baseline structure: contingency reserves for identified risks live inside the cost baseline under PM control.

prompts: You want to factor risk velocity into your response planning. Which approach best applies it?

correct.text: Plan responses earlier for high-velocity risks, since they can hit the project with little warning.
correct.why: Velocity measures how fast a risk materializes; high-velocity risks leave little reaction time.

distractors.text: Address high-probability risks first regardless of velocity, since they are likeliest to occur.
distractors.trap: Ignores velocity, the concept being applied.

distractors.text: Prioritize purely on impact magnitude, not on how quickly a risk could strike.
distractors.trap: Velocity, not impact alone, is the point.

distractors.text: Devote more resources to low-velocity risks because they affect the project longer.
distractors.trap: Low velocity allows more reaction time, not more resources.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain: risk velocity (reaction time) informs how early responses must be readied.

prompts: After you modify a risk response, new risks appear that did not exist before. What should you do next?

correct.text: Add the secondary risks to the risk register and plan responses for them.
correct.why: Secondary risks arising from responses must be identified, recorded, and actively managed.

distractors.text: Record the secondary risks but take no further action for now.
distractors.trap: Documenting without response leaves risks unmanaged.

distractors.text: Accept them because they result from the response you implemented.
distractors.trap: Consequence of a response does not make acceptance automatic.

distractors.text: Revert the response to prevent the secondary risks from arising.
distractors.trap: Abandons a valid response instead of managing its byproducts.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain: secondary risks are logged and responded to like any identified risk.

prompts: A risk trigger you were watching is observed during execution. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Assess the event and apply the response indicated by the risk management plan.
correct.why: The plan defines how to respond; the PM evaluates the situation and enacts the planned response.

distractors.text: Execute the predefined contingency plan immediately without any further analysis.
distractors.trap: Blind execution ignores whether conditions still fit the plan.

distractors.text: Reassess every project risk and update all probabilities and impacts.
distractors.trap: A single trigger does not require re-scoring the whole register.

distractors.text: Notify all stakeholders first and gather their input before responding.
distractors.trap: Delays a planned response for unnecessary consultation.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain: triggers prompt assessment and the response already defined in the risk management plan.

prompts: To counter weather delays, a construction PM rented specialized equipment to accelerate work in fair weather. The equipment introduced new safety hazards and training needs, raising cost and schedule risk. What does this illustrate?

correct.text: A secondary risk created by the implemented risk response.
correct.why: Secondary risks are new risks that arise directly from carrying out a response.

distractors.text: A residual risk remaining after the response was implemented.
distractors.trap: Residual risk is leftover exposure, not a newly created one.

distractors.text: The primary risk originally logged that prompted the response.
distractors.trap: The primary risk was the weather delay, not the new hazards.

distractors.text: A negative risk stemming from inadequate initiation-phase planning.
distractors.trap: The risk came from the response, not from early planning gaps.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain: responses can generate secondary risks that must themselves be managed.

prompts: Mid-project, the team finds a newly released framework that could speed coding and cut costs. Which response best capitalizes on this opportunity?

correct.text: Adopt the framework so the project realizes its benefits.
correct.why: Exploiting an opportunity means taking action to make sure it is captured.

distractors.text: Monitor its development and use it later only if it proves stable.
distractors.trap: This accepts or defers rather than exploits the opportunity.

distractors.text: Limit its use to avoid possible integration problems.
distractors.trap: A defensive stance forfeits most of the upside.

distractors.text: Hand adoption to an external vendor to fold into the project.
distractors.trap: Sharing surrenders control without ensuring the benefit.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain: exploit is the response that ensures a positive risk (opportunity) is realized.

prompts: During execution, unforeseen challenges have nearly exhausted the contingency reserve. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Reassess remaining risks, update the register, and request additional reserve from the sponsor.
correct.why: Depleted contingency is replenished from management reserve, which requires sponsor approval.

distractors.text: Halt all project work until the sponsor approves more funds.
distractors.trap: Suspending work is disproportionate and harms the schedule.

distractors.text: Reassess impacts and pull funds from other activities to top up the reserve.
distractors.trap: Raiding activity budgets jeopardizes that scoped work.

distractors.text: Continue as planned, assuming remaining reserve will suffice.
distractors.trap: Ignores demonstrated risk exposure and likely further overruns.

explanation: Uncertainty and cost management: exhausted contingency is replenished via management reserve through the sponsor after reassessing risk.

prompts: A single-source titanium supplier sits in an unstable region with a high probability of export bans within six months that would halt production and trigger heavy penalties. Two domestic suppliers can be qualified in twelve weeks at higher cost. Which response fits?

correct.text: Avoid the threat by starting domestic-supplier qualification now to remove reliance before restrictions hit.
correct.why: Qualifying alternatives within the window eliminates the root cause of the threat.

distractors.text: Reduce exposure by splitting orders between the current and domestic suppliers.
distractors.trap: Domestic sources are not yet qualified, so splitting is not feasible.

distractors.text: Transfer the exposure by shifting delivery-failure liability to the current supplier.
distractors.trap: Financial transfer does not keep production running.

distractors.text: Accept it, reserving funds for penalties while monitoring the situation.
distractors.trap: Acceptance is wrong given high probability and a viable alternative.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain: avoidance removes a threat's root cause when a feasible path exists within the timeline.

prompts: Members of your delivery team hold noticeably different risk attitudes. What is the best approach?

correct.text: Facilitate open discussion to understand and align their risk attitudes.
correct.why: Dialogue builds a shared risk perspective while respecting members, consistent with servant leadership.

distractors.text: Impose a single uniform risk attitude on everyone for consistency.
distractors.trap: Mandating attitudes breeds conflict and ignores context.

distractors.text: Push risk-averse members toward a risk-seeking stance to spur innovation.
distractors.trap: Forcing risk appetite can create reckless exposure.

distractors.text: Let each member keep their own attitude and decide independently.
distractors.trap: Unaligned attitudes produce inconsistent risk handling.

explanation: Team performance domain and stakeholder engagement: the PM aligns risk attitudes through facilitation, not command.

prompts: A pharma project's critical API comes from a single overseas maker; new export rules next quarter may restrict it. The ingredient has a six-month lead time and is about 44% of production cost. Which response is most suitable?

correct.text: Secure advance-purchase agreements and begin qualifying alternate suppliers in other jurisdictions while monitoring.
correct.why: It reduces both probability and impact by attacking single-source dependence proactively.

distractors.text: Accept the risk, document impact, and set aside budget reserves.
distractors.trap: Passive acceptance is wrong for a high-impact, likely threat.

distractors.text: Escalate for extra timeline and budget contingency to absorb disruption.
distractors.trap: Reactive; accommodates disruption instead of preventing it.

distractors.text: Transfer it via force-majeure clauses with the current maker.
distractors.trap: Shifts liability but production still stops if supply fails.

explanation: Uncertainty and Procurement: mitigation reduces exposure at the root by diversifying supply proactively.

prompts: A critical single-component supplier is in financial trouble, threatening your timeline. What is the most effective way to mitigate this procurement risk?

correct.text: Identify and qualify alternative suppliers while closely monitoring the current one.
correct.why: A qualified backup preserves continuity if the supplier fails, while monitoring guides timing.

distractors.text: Sign a long-term contract to secure the current supplier's commitment.
distractors.trap: Ties you tighter to an unstable source.

distractors.text: Switch to just-in-time inventory to reduce reliance on their stability.
distractors.trap: JIT cuts buffers and worsens exposure to disruption.

distractors.text: Accelerate procurement and demand early delivery of all components.
distractors.trap: Adds financial strain that may hasten the supplier's failure.

explanation: Uncertainty and Procurement: qualifying alternates reduces single-point-of-failure exposure.

prompts: Closing out a contract, you notice a minor deviation between the vendor's deliverable and the procurement plan. What should you do?

correct.text: Route it through the change control process.
correct.why: Even minor deviations are assessed through integrated change control before closure.

distractors.text: Document it and close the procurement as-is.
distractors.trap: Closing over an unassessed deviation risks later problems.

distractors.text: Have your team quietly fix it internally.
distractors.trap: Absorbs seller responsibility and hides the variance.

distractors.text: Ignore it because it is minor.
distractors.trap: Minor variances can accumulate into significant deviations.

explanation: Change control and procurement closure: deviations are evaluated through integrated change control, not waved through.

prompts: When identifying procurement risks, a key consideration is the risk of ______, which can drive up cost and delay the schedule. Which best completes this?

correct.text: Supplier default.
correct.why: A supplier failing to deliver forces costly, time-consuming scrambles for alternatives.

distractors.text: Scope creep.
distractors.trap: A scope-control risk, not a procurement risk.

distractors.text: Fixed-price contracting.
distractors.trap: A contract method, not a risk in itself.

distractors.text: Market demand.
distractors.trap: A viability factor, not a direct procurement risk.

explanation: Procurement and Uncertainty: supplier default is a core procurement risk affecting cost and schedule.

prompts: A single supplier in a politically unstable region provides most of your critical raw-material deliveries; qualifying an alternative takes six months and only nine months remain. From a procurement-risk perspective, what concept does this primarily illustrate?

correct.text: Single-source reliance creating concentration risk that needs proactive response planning.
correct.why: One supplier for most deliveries plus regional instability is textbook concentration risk.

distractors.text: Geographic diversification concerns to address through renegotiation and penalty clauses.
distractors.trap: Penalties treat symptoms, not the concentration root cause.

distractors.text: Supply-chain vulnerability that requires accelerating backup-supplier qualification.
distractors.trap: Describes a response, not the concept being illustrated.

distractors.text: Supplier relationship management needing enhanced communication protocols.
distractors.trap: Communication won't fix a structural single-source exposure.

explanation: Uncertainty/risk domain: over-reliance on one source is concentration risk calling for proactive response planning.

prompts: You are managing an international project where a critical component is delivered from another country, and you are concerned about import/export restrictions. What should be your course of action?

correct.text: Research the relevant import/export regulations and incorporate them into the procurement management plan.
correct.why: Proactively planning for legal constraints protects schedule, budget, and compliance.

distractors.text: Delay the procurement process until you have more information.
distractors.trap: Reactive delay instead of planning ahead.

distractors.text: Assume the exporting country will not impose restrictions.
distractors.trap: Assumption invites penalties and delays.

distractors.text: Ask the supplier to provide the restriction details.
distractors.trap: The supplier may not know your country's import rules.

explanation: Business Environment / compliance: identify legal and regulatory requirements and plan for them proactively.

prompts: Your community-center project depends heavily on one brick supplier now facing labor disputes, and no contingency plan exists for this risk. What should your next course of action be?

correct.text: Establish and activate a contingency plan, including sourcing alternative brick suppliers.
correct.why: A proactive contingency keeps the project on track despite the supplier disruption.

distractors.text: Cancel the order and search for a new supplier immediately.
distractors.trap: Reactive; may introduce new risk and delay.

distractors.text: Wait for the supplier to resolve the dispute and delay the project if needed.
distractors.trap: Passive acceptance of schedule slippage.

distractors.text: Reduce the project scope to match the reduced brick supply.
distractors.trap: Compromises objectives and stakeholder expectations.

explanation: Uncertainty/risk domain: respond to an emerging threat by developing and activating a contingency plan.

topic: Hedging currency risk via contract type
prompts: Procuring components from an international supplier, a project manager sees that exchange-rate fluctuations could hit the budget. Which response best reduces this procurement risk?

correct.text: Use a fixed-price contract with the supplier to lock in the cost against exchange-rate swings.
correct.why: A fixed price transfers currency-fluctuation risk to the seller and stabilizes the budget.

distractors.text: Use a cost-reimbursable contract to allow flexibility with rate changes.
distractors.trap: Flexibility exposes the budget to the very fluctuation.

distractors.text: Increase the project budget to absorb potential exchange-rate losses.
distractors.trap: Funds the risk instead of reducing it.

distractors.text: Diversify suppliers across different countries to spread currency risk.
distractors.trap: Spreads but does not eliminate currency exposure.

explanation: Uncertainty/risk domain: contract type is a risk-response tool; a fixed price transfers cost/currency risk to the seller.

topic: Integrated change control for supplier delay
prompts: On a multinational infrastructure project, a critical equipment supplier notifies you of an unforeseen delivery delay that threatens the critical path and could cascade across work packages. Your Procurement Management Plan defines change-control protocols. What is the most suitable course of action?

correct.text: Initiate integrated change control to weigh options such as rescheduling or an alternative supplier, with key stakeholders.
correct.why: A cross-cutting delay flows through integrated change control, not ad hoc action.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor for extra funds to cover express delivery from another supplier.
distractors.trap: Jumps to a solution before analysis.

distractors.text: Ask the current supplier for a discount to indirectly offset the delay.
distractors.trap: Money does not recover the critical path.

distractors.text: Directly contact an alternative supplier to expedite ordering without the change board.
distractors.trap: Bypasses the governed change process.

explanation: Project work / change: schedule-impacting supplier changes are evaluated through integrated change control with stakeholders.

prompts: In evaluating a project, what is the primary purpose of performing a sensitivity analysis?

correct.text: To assess how changes in individual input variables affect project outcomes.
correct.why: Sensitivity analysis isolates which variables most influence outcomes, revealing key risks and uncertainties.

distractors.text: To calculate net present value from projected cash flows over time.
distractors.trap: Describes NPV, a return calculation, not sensitivity analysis.

distractors.text: To rank evaluation criteria using pairwise comparisons.
distractors.trap: Describes the Analytic Hierarchy Process, not sensitivity analysis.

distractors.text: To optimize resource allocation across a portfolio of projects.
distractors.trap: Describes portfolio optimization, not sensitivity analysis.

explanation: Aligns with the Uncertainty performance domain—sensitivity analysis is a quantitative risk technique identifying the variables driving outcomes.

prompts: While reviewing an engineering project you discover that certain prohibited hazardous materials have made their way into the work. What is the best response?

correct.text: Halt use of the materials, assess impact on scope, schedule, and cost, and plan compliant substitutes.
correct.why: Compliance and safety come first; stop the exposure, then evaluate impacts and plan a remedy.

distractors.text: Wait for stakeholders to raise a concern before acting.
distractors.trap: Passive; ignores the compliance and safety duty to act now.

distractors.text: Continue as-is because replacing the materials raises cost.
distractors.trap: Prioritizes cost over legal and safety compliance.

distractors.text: Swap in alternatives immediately without assessing project impact.
distractors.trap: Acts before understanding scope, schedule, and cost effects.

explanation: Supports the Business Environment domain's compliance emphasis—safety and regulatory compliance are non-negotiable and precede other objectives.

topic: Integrated change control for procurement change
prompts: Midway through a complex infrastructure project, a vendor discontinues a key component; the team proposes a newer model that fits budget and schedule but may need design tweaks. Procurement emails rough impacts asking for quick approval before stock runs out. How should the project manager proceed?

correct.text: Submit a formal change request for full impact analysis and Change Control Board routing.
correct.why: Changes affecting baselines must be documented, analyzed, and authorized before implementation.

distractors.text: Get verbal sponsor approval to proceed, then inform the team.
distractors.trap: Verbal approval skips required impact analysis and documentation.

distractors.text: Update the schedule baseline for the tweaks and tell stakeholders later.
distractors.trap: Baselines change only after an authorized change request.

distractors.text: Authorize the purchase contingent on warranty and file the decision.
distractors.trap: Buying first bypasses integrated change control.

explanation: Reflects Perform Integrated Change Control (Delivery/Planning)—baseline-affecting changes require formal analysis and authorization.

topic: Assess impact before acting on proposed change
prompts: In a meeting, a team member proposes a scope change that would boost stakeholder satisfaction. Which action should come first?

correct.text: Assess the change's impact on scope, schedule, and cost.
correct.why: Understanding impacts first enables an informed decision through change control.

distractors.text: Discuss the change with stakeholders before assessing its impact.
distractors.trap: Engaging before analysis skips the needed impact assessment.

distractors.text: Authorize it immediately to keep the team motivated.
distractors.trap: Approving without analysis ignores potential negative impacts.

distractors.text: Dismiss the suggestion to protect progress.
distractors.trap: Ignoring input may lose value and demotivate the team.

explanation: Reflects Perform Integrated Change Control—evaluate impact before deciding on any proposed change.

topic: Assess impact of new regulation first
prompts: You learn that new regulatory compliance requirements will affect your project. Which action should come first?

correct.text: Assess the impact on scope, schedule, and cost.
correct.why: Quantifying implications first lets you propose informed adjustments and next steps.

distractors.text: Revise the project management plan right away.
distractors.trap: Updating plans precedes understanding the impact.

distractors.text: Inform the executive sponsor immediately.
distractors.trap: Report with an understood impact, not before analysis.

distractors.text: Submit a change request at once.
distractors.trap: Change requests follow once the impact is known.

explanation: Reflects the Business Environment domain—understand a compliance change's impact before acting on it.

prompts: In project governance, which element provides the framework by which project decisions are made?

correct.text: The governance structure.
correct.why: The governance structure defines authorities and the framework through which project decisions are made.

distractors.text: The risk management plan.
distractors.trap: Defines risk handling, not the overall decision framework.

distractors.text: The stakeholder engagement plan.
distractors.trap: Guides engagement, not decision authority.

distractors.text: The communication plan.
distractors.trap: Defines information flow, not decision-making authority.

explanation: Reflects the Business Environment domain—the governance structure frames decision-making and oversight.

prompts: During a CRM sprint review, users expect automated report generation, but the scope baseline doesn't include it. The sponsor recalls it was discussed informally early on but never captured. The project is on schedule and budget. What is the best action?

correct.text: Document it as a change request and submit it through integrated change control.
correct.why: Work not in the baseline is unauthorized; only change control can properly evaluate and approve it.

distractors.text: Treat it as a requirements clarification and update the scope baseline.
distractors.trap: It was never captured, so it isn't a clarification of existing scope.

distractors.text: Add it to the current sprint since the sponsor confirmed the discussion.
distractors.trap: Informal discussion doesn't authorize scope; this is scope creep.

distractors.text: Route it through change control while starting preliminary design now.
distractors.trap: Beginning work before approval undermines the process.

explanation: Reflects Perform Integrated Change Control—unbaselined scope requires a formal change request, not informal addition.

topic: Handling demand to reverse approved change
prompts: On an office-building project, a stakeholder is unhappy with an already-approved change request and demands it be withdrawn. What is your next step?

correct.text: Explain the change management process and point to the approved project management plan.
correct.why: An authorized change is part of the plan; reversing it requires another change request, not a demand.

distractors.text: Accept the demand and reverse the change immediately.
distractors.trap: Undermines the established change control process.

distractors.text: Halt all work until the dispute is resolved.
distractors.trap: Causes needless delay and cost.

distractors.text: Ignore the stakeholder and proceed as planned.
distractors.trap: Disengaging risks stakeholder buy-in and project success.

explanation: Reflects Perform Integrated Change Control and Stakeholder engagement—reversing an approved change requires the formal process.

prompts: Which document defines how changes are identified, classified, authorized, documented, and communicated on a project?

correct.text: The change management plan.
correct.why: It sets the processes and authorities for handling project changes throughout the project.

distractors.text: The risk management plan.
distractors.trap: Covers risk handling, not change procedures.

distractors.text: The stakeholder communications approach.
distractors.trap: Governs communication, not change control.

distractors.text: The quality management plan.
distractors.trap: Covers quality requirements, not change management.

explanation: Reflects Planning—the change management plan governs the integrated change control process.

prompts: During validation of a new patient-records system, nursing staff report that reaching critical allergy information takes five clicks—unsafe in emergencies—and the nursing director submits a change request to reduce it to two. You've documented it and know it touches the UI, database queries, and training. The project is three weeks ahead with 10.5% contingency. How should you handle the request?

correct.text: Perform an integrated impact assessment across all affected areas and prepare recommendations for the change control board.
correct.why: Baseline-affecting changes need full cross-area impact analysis and CCB review before authorization.

distractors.text: Assess only the schedule impact and take it to the sponsor for expedited approval.
distractors.trap: Narrow analysis and bypassing the CCB skip full impact review.

distractors.text: Check feasibility with developers and implement using available float and contingency.
distractors.trap: Implements before authorized change control.

distractors.text: Fast-track the UI change for safety and document it afterward.
distractors.trap: Acting first and documenting later bypasses change control.

explanation: Reflects Perform Integrated Change Control—assess impacts across all knowledge areas and route to the CCB, even for safety-driven changes.

prompts: A large ERP rollout is in the data-migration phase when regulators flag the encryption approach as potentially non-conformant. The sponsor presses the project manager to keep migrating to hit quarter targets and fix compliance later. Under the project's governance framework, what should the project manager do?

correct.text: Escalate the compliance issue to the governance board for formal direction before continuing migration.
correct.why: Regulatory non-conformance is a governance matter; PMBOK requires transparent escalation and authorized direction over schedule pressure.

distractors.text: Use contingency to install temporary encryption, keep migrating, and plan a permanent fix after go-live.
distractors.trap: Proceeding on a workaround bypasses governance and accepts a known breach.

distractors.text: Decide with the sponsor alone, log it in the risk register, and hold the schedule.
distractors.trap: Sponsor-only decisions skip the authority governance assigns compliance issues.

distractors.text: Have the technical lead and security analyst quietly rewrite the procedures as an internal tweak.
distractors.trap: Treating a regulatory flag as a minor edit hides it from oversight.

explanation: Illustrates the Stewardship principle in the Business Environment: regulatory compliance issues are escalated to governance rather than subordinated to schedule.

topic: Route new requirement through change control
prompts: Two months into a global payroll project, auditors announce a new statutory filing requirement adding several reports. The sponsor wants quick confirmation the project can still finish on time. What should the project manager do first?

correct.text: Document the requirement and submit it through integrated change control for impact assessment.
correct.why: New scope after baselines is a change request; PMBOK evaluates impact via integrated change control before any commitment.

distractors.text: Approve the addition, update the scope baseline, then brief the change board afterward.
distractors.trap: Approving before assessment bypasses control and exceeds PM authority.

distractors.text: Update the risk register and project plan to reflect the new requirement.
distractors.trap: Plans change only after the request is authorized, not before.

distractors.text: Run a rapid cost and schedule re-estimate before logging it with the board.
distractors.trap: Detailed analysis follows formally logging the change, not precedes it.

explanation: Reflects Business Environment governance and integrated change control: log and assess impact before committing.

topic: Execute planned risk response when a risk trigger occurs
prompts: During construction of a new headquarters, your weather consultant warns of a high likelihood of a major storm within three months. Your risk register already lists severe weather with planned responses: temporary reinforcements and schedule buffers. What should you recommend?

correct.text: Activate the planned responses, increase monitoring, and communicate updated timelines to stakeholders.
correct.why: A trigger on a documented risk calls for executing the pre-planned response, not devising new ones from scratch.

distractors.text: Reassess the scores and run a workshop to build entirely new response strategies.
distractors.trap: Rebuilding responses wastes time when workable ones already exist.

distractors.text: Buy delay insurance to transfer the risk and keep the original schedule.
distractors.trap: Insurance covers cost, not physical safety or schedule; insufficient alone.

distractors.text: Escalate to the oversight panel to decide whether to suspend all outdoor work.
distractors.trap: Suspending everything over-avoids a risk with responses already prepared.

explanation: Reflects the Uncertainty performance domain: execute the planned risk response when its trigger occurs.

prompts: Your project depends on a critical component from a single vendor that may deliver late and cause major delays. To reduce this exposure, you contract a second supplier for the same component despite the added cost. Which risk response strategy is this?

correct.text: Mitigate: reduce the probability or impact of the threat.
correct.why: Adding a second source lowers the chance a single vendor's failure delays the project, the definition of mitigation.

distractors.text: Accept the risk and prepare a contingency plan for delays.
distractors.trap: Acceptance takes no proactive action; you actively added a supplier.

distractors.text: Transfer the risk by outsourcing the whole project to another firm.
distractors.trap: Transfer shifts ownership to a third party; you kept and reduced it.

distractors.text: Avoid the risk by eliminating the need for the component.
distractors.trap: Avoidance removes the cause entirely; the component is still required.

explanation: Reflects the Uncertainty performance domain: correctly classify threat responses (mitigation).

topic: Authority to approve the project charter
prompts: Who holds the authority to approve and authorize the project charter?

correct.text: The project sponsor or senior management, who have the authority to commit organizational resources and formally authorize the project.
correct.why: Only the sponsor or senior management can commit the organizational resources needed to execute the project, giving them the authority to approve the charter.

distractors.text: The project team, who will perform the work and therefore understand what resources and activities the charter needs to authorize.
distractors.trap: The team executes chartered work but lacks the organizational authority to approve resource commitments.

distractors.text: The project manager, who coordinates all project activities and typically develops the charter document on behalf of leadership.
distractors.trap: The PM usually drafts the charter but does not hold authority to approve the resource commitments it requires.

distractors.text: The customer, who defines the business requirements and will ultimately judge whether project deliverables meet their expectations.
distractors.trap: The customer defines requirements and accepts deliverables but does not hold internal authority to authorize organizational resource commitments.

explanation: Reflects Business Environment governance: the sponsor or senior management authorizes the charter.

topic: Assess change impact then consult client
prompts: Mid-execution, a team member proposes an innovative enhancement that could add functionality but may extend the timeline and budget. The client doesn't know about it. What is the best next step?

correct.text: Assess the impact on scope, schedule, and cost, then discuss it with the client.
correct.why: Understanding impacts before consulting the client keeps change control disciplined and communication transparent.

distractors.text: Send it to the change control board for approval before reviewing implications.
distractors.trap: The board needs an impact analysis first, not a raw idea.

distractors.text: Thank the member but proceed without it to avoid delays.
distractors.trap: Dismissing ideas outright stifles beneficial improvement.

distractors.text: Implement the change immediately to show responsiveness.
distractors.trap: Acting without assessment invites uncontrolled cost and schedule overrun.

explanation: Reflects Change Control in the Business Environment: assess impact before authorizing or consulting.

prompts: During execution, the client's CEO verbally tells your site supervisor to add an executive lounge; the eager supervisor starts work, and two days later materials are ordered and demolition begun, none of it in the contract or scope baseline. What is the best action?

correct.text: Halt the unauthorized work, document it, and raise a formal change request through integrated change control.
correct.why: Uncontrolled scope must stop and route through change control so impacts are assessed and properly authorized.

distractors.text: Halt the work, cost it, present a revised budget to the CEO, and update the baseline.
distractors.trap: Updating the baseline before authorization violates change control.

distractors.text: Continue, since the CEO speaks for the client and can authorize scope.
distractors.trap: Verbal CEO direction isn't formal change authorization.

distractors.text: Record the verbal instruction as retroactive approval and update the WBS.
distractors.trap: Retroactive documentation still bypasses required formal processing.

explanation: Reflects Change Control governance: stop uncontrolled changes and use integrated change control.

topic: Formal change control with trade-off options
prompts: On a healthcare IT project, the client asks to pull features planned for a later phase into the next release; your analysis shows this needs roughly 22% more development hours. What is the best approach?

correct.text: Assess the impact on constraints, log a formal change request, and present the client trade-off options.
correct.why: Integrated change control quantifies impact and lets the client make an informed decision.

distractors.text: Fold the features in now since they were already planned, reallocating people from lower-priority tasks.
distractors.trap: Absorbs a scope change informally without assessing true impact.

distractors.text: Extend the timeline by the same percentage as the added work and proceed.
distractors.trap: Assumes hours map linearly to schedule and decides for the client.

distractors.text: Tell the client it would hurt quality and offer a client-funded parallel track.
distractors.trap: Presumes a solution instead of analyzing and presenting options.

explanation: Change control / stakeholder engagement — route scope changes through integrated change control and give stakeholders informed choices.

topic: Integrated change request for interconnected issues
prompts: Defect fixes will add eight days to the schedule while, at the same time, a critical vendor requests a contract modification over supply-chain issues; the sponsor stresses staying aligned with strategy. As the integration lead, what should you do?

correct.text: Analyze the combined impact on the integrated project plan and prepare one comprehensive change request for CCB review.
correct.why: Interconnected baseline changes should be assessed together and routed through integrated change control.

distractors.text: Analyze the combined impact, then escalate both issues to the sponsor for a decision.
distractors.trap: Bypasses change-control governance and offloads the PM's analysis.

distractors.text: Log each issue separately and have functional managers develop isolated corrective actions.
distractors.trap: Siloed handling misses the cross-functional dependencies.

distractors.text: Meet with the quality and procurement leads to update the risk log before assessing baselines.
distractors.trap: A useful interim step, but it does not resolve the baseline changes.

explanation: Integration / change control — baseline-affecting changes flow through one integrated change request to the CCB.

prompts: Which project artifact is used to document and track all authorized changes to the project baseline?

correct.text: Change log
correct.why: The change log records each change's status, disposition, and impact across the project.

distractors.text: Change request form
distractors.trap: Initiates a single change; not the running record of all changes.

distractors.text: Project charter
distractors.trap: Authorizes the project; it does not track ongoing changes.

distractors.text: Work breakdown structure
distractors.trap: Organizes scope, not change records.

explanation: Change control — the change log provides transparency and traceability of authorized changes.

prompts: Your procurement specialist reports that the sole vendor for a bespoke component is hitting production delays that could push out the critical path. What is the most effective response?

correct.text: Reassess the schedule impact and develop mitigation options, possibly including alternative vendors.
correct.why: Analyzing impact and planning responses proactively manages the threat.

distractors.text: Accept the delay and just inform stakeholders without exploring options.
distractors.trap: Passive acceptance forgoes available mitigation.

distractors.text: Push straight to a contract penalty clause without assessing schedule impact.
distractors.trap: Jumps to a remedy before understanding the impact.

distractors.text: Tell procurement to expedite delivery by any means necessary.
distractors.trap: Ignores consequences to cost, quality, and relationships.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — analyze the threat and plan a proportionate risk response.

prompts: In a risk review you find several implemented responses aren't reducing their risks and the team has surfaced new risks. Per the PMBOK Guide, what is the best next step?

correct.text: Update the risk register, reassess risks, and adjust risk responses as needed.
correct.why: Monitoring risk means re-evaluating exposure and adapting responses continuously.

distractors.text: Assume current responses will eventually work and focus on deadlines.
distractors.trap: Ignoring ineffective responses lets exposure grow.

distractors.text: Prioritize deliverables now and handle the risks after they're done.
distractors.trap: Deferring risk work invites materialization.

distractors.text: Ask senior management for more resources to cover the emerging risks.
distractors.trap: Requests resources before analyzing what's actually failing.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — continuously monitor and adapt risk responses.

topic: Reassess risk register on market change
prompts: Recent market swings have sharply raised material costs, making your original risk assessments obsolete and threatening budget and schedule. What is the most appropriate step regarding the risk register?

correct.text: Revise the register by reassessing affected risks' probabilities and impacts.
correct.why: Reassessment keeps the register aligned with the changed risk profile for sound decisions.

distractors.text: Add new risks but leave the existing assessments unchanged.
distractors.trap: Ignores that existing risks now have different exposure.

distractors.text: Wait to update until the cost impacts are fully realized.
distractors.trap: Delaying updates undermines proactive risk management.

distractors.text: Ask stakeholders how to change the plan before touching the register.
distractors.trap: Reverses the order; update the register to inform decisions.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — keep the risk register current as conditions change.

prompts: During which process does the project manager continuously track risks to detect changes in their status or severity?

correct.text: Monitor Risks
correct.why: Monitor Risks tracks identified risks, spots new ones, and evaluates response effectiveness.

distractors.text: Identify Risks
distractors.trap: Finds and documents risks; it precedes monitoring.

distractors.text: Plan Risk Responses
distractors.trap: Develops responses before monitoring begins.

distractors.text: Perform Qualitative Risk Analysis
distractors.trap: Prioritizes risks; not ongoing status tracking.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — Monitor Risks maintains awareness of changing exposure.

prompts: In risk management, how do a contingency plan and a fallback plan differ?

correct.text: A contingency plan is enacted if the risk occurs; a fallback plan is used if the primary response fails.
correct.why: Contingency is the planned response to a risk; fallback is the backup if that response is inadequate.

distractors.text: A contingency plan and a fallback plan are interchangeable terms; both describe a pre-planned response that activates when a specific risk event materializes.
distractors.trap: They are not identical — they serve distinct, sequential roles in the risk response hierarchy.

distractors.text: A contingency plan is a proactive mitigation action taken before the risk event occurs, while a fallback plan is an avoidance strategy applied if the threat escalates.
distractors.trap: Conflates mitigation and avoidance strategies with these specific plan types, reversing their actual roles.

distractors.text: A contingency plan eliminates the risk by changing the project approach, while a fallback plan reduces residual impact by scaling back the scope of response.
distractors.trap: Mischaracterizes both plans by assigning avoidance and mitigation roles to them rather than their sequential response roles.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — layered risk responses: contingency first, then fallback.

prompts: In sprint planning, your DevOps engineer mentions the cloud provider is announcing changes to its container platform that land before go-live; the product owner wants to keep building features while the architect flags possible pipeline compatibility issues. What is the most appropriate action?

correct.text: Log the potential risk in the register and schedule a technical assessment of the changes' impact on the pipeline.
correct.why: It records the risk and gathers analysis before deciding a response, without halting progress.

distractors.text: Record it as a known issue in the status report and have the architect scope adjustments.
distractors.trap: Misclassifies an uncertain future event as a current issue.

distractors.text: Keep the current sprint priorities and just have the engineer monitor provider notices.
distractors.trap: Too passive for a foreseeable, dated threat.

distractors.text: Escalate to the sponsor and request extra budget before any technical analysis.
distractors.trap: Requests resources before understanding the actual need.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — identify, document, and analyze emerging risks proactively.

prompts: During risk monitoring, which activity involves regularly reviewing the risk register and response plans to confirm they remain relevant and effective?

correct.text: Updating the risk register and response plans
correct.why: Ongoing review keeps risk information and responses current as risks change and emerge.

distractors.text: Reviewing the stakeholder engagement plan
distractors.trap: Relates to stakeholders, not risk monitoring.

distractors.text: Conducting qualitative risk analysis
distractors.trap: Done during analysis, earlier than monitoring.

distractors.text: Developing risk thresholds
distractors.trap: Set when planning the risk approach, not during monitoring.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — Monitor Risks keeps the register and responses effective.

prompts: Your team finds that assigning your most capable engineers to a key task could let the project finish early, and you want to make sure the project actually captures this benefit. Which risk response strategy is this?

correct.text: Exploit
correct.why: Exploit takes definite action to ensure a positive risk is realized.

distractors.text: Enhance
distractors.trap: Enhance raises the odds of an opportunity but doesn't guarantee it.

distractors.text: Share
distractors.trap: Share allocates the opportunity to a third party best able to capture it.

distractors.text: Accept
distractors.trap: Accept takes no proactive action to pursue the opportunity.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — exploit is the aggressive response that ensures an opportunity occurs.

prompts: Mid-migration, the primary database can't transfer critical customer data because of unforeseen corruption. Your fallback plan is to restore a two-week-old backup and re-enter recent records. What is the most appropriate next step?

correct.text: Activate the fallback plan and inform stakeholders of the revised timeline.
correct.why: The primary response failed, so enacting the fallback and communicating impact is warranted.

distractors.text: Keep trying to repair the corrupted database and delay the fallback until repair succeeds.
distractors.trap: Gambles on repair while data remains unavailable.

distractors.text: Pause and brainstorm new alternatives, holding the fallback in reserve.
distractors.trap: Delays a ready, proven response the situation calls for.

distractors.text: Continue migrating from the corrupted database to salvage what you can.
distractors.trap: Risks propagating corrupt data.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — trigger the fallback when the primary response is exhausted, and keep stakeholders informed.

prompts: At a phase-end lessons learned session, you want to use historical risk data to improve future planning. Which risk register attribute is most valuable for that?

correct.text: Actual outcomes and effectiveness of the implemented risk responses
correct.why: Comparing what happened and which responses worked yields the most actionable lessons.

distractors.text: Initial probability and impact assessments made during planning
distractors.trap: Shows estimates, not accuracy or outcomes.

distractors.text: Names of the stakeholders who first reported each risk
distractors.trap: Administrative detail with little learning value.

distractors.text: The chronological order in which risks were logged
distractors.trap: Sequencing offers minimal insight for future planning.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — response effectiveness feeds organizational process assets and future risk planning.

prompts: A Risk Breakdown Structure (RBS) is a hierarchical representation of what?

correct.text: Potential sources of risk
correct.why: The RBS organizes and categorizes the sources of risk that could affect a project.

distractors.text: Project deliverables
distractors.trap: Deliverables belong in the WBS, not the RBS.

distractors.text: Project tasks
distractors.trap: Work items are decomposed in the WBS.

distractors.text: Project resources
distractors.trap: Resources are handled in resource planning.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — the RBS categorizes risk sources for systematic identification.

prompts: Risk exposure has climbed steadily across three sprints despite active risk responses, and the sponsor wants a project-health update. What should the project manager do?

correct.text: Analyze why current responses aren't working and, with the team, decide what adjustments could reverse the trend.
correct.why: Understanding root cause and adapting responses addresses rising exposure directly.

distractors.text: Note the trend in the status report and keep monitoring to see if it stabilizes.
distractors.trap: A clear three-sprint trend needs action, not more watching.

distractors.text: Show the sponsor the trend and request additional contingency reserves.
distractors.trap: Asks for resources before diagnosing why responses fail.

distractors.text: Escalate to the oversight board and pause work for an external reassessment.
distractors.trap: Overreacts before trying internal analysis.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — inspect and adapt risk responses when exposure trends the wrong way.

prompts: You have both a contingency plan and a fallback plan for a critical vendor-dependency risk. In execution the contingency plan proves ineffective. Which statement best describes how the fallback plan relates and when it's used?

correct.text: The fallback is a secondary response used when the contingency plan fails to adequately handle the risk's impact.
correct.why: Fallback is held in reserve and triggered only after the contingency response proves insufficient.

distractors.text: The fallback works alongside the contingency, each addressing different aspects of the same risk.
distractors.trap: They aren't concurrent; fallback follows a failed contingency.

distractors.text: The fallback is evaluated at the same time as the contingency to pick the more cost-efficient option.
distractors.trap: They aren't side-by-side alternatives during execution.

distractors.text: The fallback is assessed before the contingency to set baseline conditions.
distractors.trap: Reverses the actual sequence.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — contingency responds first; fallback is the reserved backup.

prompts: Conducting qualitative risk analysis, you have subjective likelihood and impact ratings from SMEs and need to visually compare risks to support prioritization. Which tool fits best?

correct.text: A probability and impact matrix plotting each risk by assessed likelihood and severity
correct.why: The P-I matrix is the standard qualitative tool for visually comparing and prioritizing risks.

distractors.text: A Monte Carlo simulation running thousands of iterations of project outcomes
distractors.trap: A quantitative technique needing numerical data.

distractors.text: An expected monetary value calculation for each risk
distractors.trap: Quantitative, used in decision-tree analysis.

distractors.text: A sensitivity (tornado) diagram of variables driving cost variance
distractors.trap: Quantitative and requires numerical correlation data.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — the P-I matrix supports qualitative risk prioritization.

prompts: In quantitative risk analysis, what is a Monte Carlo simulation?

correct.text: A mathematical technique that runs many random samples to model system behavior under uncertainty.
correct.why: It aggregates numerous random iterations to estimate ranges of possible outcomes.

distractors.text: A contingency-planning technique that lists alternative actions if risks occur.
distractors.trap: Confuses simulation with response planning.

distractors.text: A risk technique focused on avoiding potential risks.
distractors.trap: Avoidance is a response strategy, not a simulation.

distractors.text: A qualitative technique that rates the likelihood and impact of risks.
distractors.trap: Monte Carlo is quantitative, not qualitative.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — Monte Carlo is a quantitative modeling technique.

prompts: In a sensitivity analysis, fuel prices show an index of 0.88, driver wages 0.55, and warehouse rent 0.32. How should you explain the practical meaning of the 0.88 to stakeholders?

correct.text: Fuel-price changes would cause proportionally larger swings in project outcomes than the other variables.
correct.why: A higher sensitivity index means outcomes respond more strongly to that variable.

distractors.text: Fuel prices make up 88% of the transportation budget.
distractors.trap: Misreads the index as a budget share.

distractors.text: Fuel prices have been stable and need little contingency.
distractors.trap: Inverts the meaning; high sensitivity signals more attention.

distractors.text: Fuel-price changes would cause proportionally smaller outcome swings than others.
distractors.trap: Contradicts what a high index indicates.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — sensitivity analysis ranks which variables most influence outcomes.

prompts: A third-party API your system depends on has had intermittent outages for two weeks; the vendor hasn't acknowledged it and QA reports little testing impact so far. Your business analyst suggests waiting. What is the most appropriate action?

correct.text: Log the API reliability concern in the risk register and meet with the vendor about SLA commitments and contingency options.
correct.why: It tracks the emerging risk and engages the source proportionately before it escalates.

distractors.text: Log it in the issue tracker and have the team research alternative providers now.
distractors.trap: Misclassifies a risk as an issue and jumps to alternatives prematurely.

distractors.text: Run a full risk-assessment workshop with all stakeholders and design a system-wide backup integration.
distractors.trap: Disproportionate to a low-impact, intermittent problem.

distractors.text: Keep monitoring API metrics and have QA track delays before escalating.
distractors.trap: Too passive for a known, growing dependency risk.

explanation: Uncertainty performance domain — identify and engage emerging risks proportionately and proactively.

prompts: An infrastructure upgrade is underway when specialized equipment fails and the primary repair plan does not work, threatening the schedule.
